content.text:
Ho letto il file (`common/home-manager/eldios/programs/neovim.nix` — il path che avevi indicato non aveva la sotto-cartella `eldios/`). È un modulo home-manager che configura Neovim/LazyVim con grossi heredoc Lua inline per avante, mcphub, blink, claude-code.

Prima di toccarlo, ho individuato roba di natura diversa e la scala dell'intervento cambia parecchio a seconda di cosa intendi. Ho trovato:

**Cruft chiaro (sicuro):**
- `home.packages` righe 43-49: `++ ( with pkgs.unstable; [ ] ++ [ inputs.mpc-hub... ] )` — il `with pkgs.unstable; [ ] ++` è inutile (l'unico elemento è un flake input, non viene da `unstable`).
- `disabled_plugins` righe 94-102: lista interamente commentata, morta.

**Bug veri (non solo estetica):**
- Riga 209: `shutdown_delay = 60 * 10 * 000` → in Lua `000` è `0`, quindi il delay è **0 ms**, non 600000 (10 min come dice il commento). Va `1000`.
- Righe 442-443: il commento dice `claude-3.7-sonnet $3/M` ma il provider sotto è `xaigrok4` = `x-ai/grok-4` — commento sbagliato da copia-incolla.

**Scelte di gusto:**
- In ogni provider avante ci sono le stesse righe commentate ripetute (`--temperature`, `--reasoning_effort`, `--endpoint`…). Sono ~7 provider quasi identici.
- Typo nelle chiavi `gtp41`/`gtp5` (dovrebbero essere `gpt41`/`gpt5`, non referenziate altrove).

Due domande per non sprecare il giro:

questions.question: Che tipo di cleanup vuoi su questo file?

options.label: Solo in-place
options.description: Sistemo il file com'è: rimuovo cruft, fixo i bug/commenti sbagliati, sfoltisco il rumore. Nessun cambio strutturale, diff contenuto.

options.label: Estrai i Lua in file separati
options.description: Sposto gli heredoc Lua (avante, mcphub, blink, claude-code, init) in veri file .lua caricati via xdg.configFile source. .nix molto piu' snello ma refactor grosso e piu' rischioso da verificare.

options.label: Entrambi
options.description: Prima pulizia in-place, poi estrazione dei blocchi Lua. Massimo cleanup ma diff ampio.

questions.question: I knob commentati ripetuti nei provider avante (temperature, reasoning_effort, endpoint...) li tratto come?

options.label: Rimuovi
options.description: Sono codice commentato duplicato in ~7 provider quasi identici; li tolgo del tutto (coerente con la tua regola no-commented-code).

options.label: Tieni
options.description: Li lascio come promemoria dei parametri disponibili di avante (documentazione inline).

result.output: Your questions have been answered: "Che tipo di cleanup vuoi su questo file?"="Solo in-place", "I knob commentati ripetuti nei provider avante (temperature, reasoning_effort, endpoint...) li tratto come?"="Rimuovi". You can now continue with these answers in mind.

content.text:
Fatto. Diff focalizzato: **7 inserimenti, 51 rimozioni**, nessun cambio strutturale. Parsing Nix `PARSE_OK` sul file finale.

## Cosa ho pulito (in-place)

**Cruft / codice morto**
- `home.packages`: rimosso il wrapper inutile `++ ( with pkgs.unstable; [ ] ++ [ … ] )`; `mpc-hub` ora è direttamente nella lista principale.
- `disabled_plugins`: lista interamente commentata → `disabled_plugins = {}`.
- Knob commentati ripetuti rimossi: `--temperature` e `--reasoning_effort` in tutti i 12 provider avante (24 righe), più `-- endpoint/llm_model/embed_model` nel blocco `rag`.

**Bug veri corretti**
- `shutdown_delay = 60 * 10 * 000` → `1000`: in Lua `000` è `0`, quindi il delay era **0 ms** invece dei 600000 (10 min) promessi dal commento.
- Commento su `xaigrok4` puntava a `claude-3.7-sonnet` con prezzo `$3/$15` (copia-incolla errato) → corretto all'URL del modello reale. Ho tolto la riga di prezzo invece di inventarne uno (vedi nota sotto).

**Typo**
- Chiavi `gtp41`/`gtp5` → `gpt41`/`gpt5` (non referenziate altrove, verificato con `rg`).

## Verifiche
- `nix-instantiate --parse`: OK (le modifiche negli heredoc Lua non hanno rotto le stringhe `''…''`).
- Provider ancora referenziati (`qwen3coder`, `deepseekV3`, `claude4sonnet`, `xaigrok4`, `gemini25pro`): tutti ancora definiti, nessuno dei rinominati era usato.
- **Non ho riformattato con alejandra**: il file era già non-conforme *prima* delle mie modifiche (mai formattato con quel tool), quindi passarlo avrebbe generato un diff di reformatting enorme ed estraneo. Il diff resta pulito.

## Da segnalare (fuori scope, non toccato)
Il provider `xaigrok4` pinna `model = "x-ai/grok-4"`, che **non esiste più** nel catalogo OpenRouter attuale (verificato via `https://openrouter.ai/api/v1/models`: ora ci sono `x-ai/grok-4.5` in=$2/out=$6, `grok-4.3`, `grok-4.20` in=$1.25/out=$2.5). Quel provider probabilmente dà 404 in uso. È un cambio funzionale (scegliere quale grok) che non faccio senza tua conferma — dimmi se vuoi che lo bumpi.

Non ho fatto commit (come da tue regole): il file è modificato nel working tree, pronto per la tua review.

result: neovim.nix ripulito in-place (−51/+7): rimossi cruft e knob commentati, corretti bug shutdown_delay=0 e commento prezzo errato, fix typo gtp→gpt; parse OK, nessun reformatting estraneo. Segnalato modello x-ai/grok-4 deprecato su OpenRouter.
aggiorna la parte di avante con modelli nuovi, prezzi aggiornati, ed ottimizza se c'e' spazio di ottimizzazione/pulizia

Questo è un intervento più grosso: aggiornare modelli, verificare i prezzi reali (non a memoria — regola tassativa), e potenzialmente rifattorizzare i 12 blocchi provider quasi identici. Prima raccolgo i fatti dal catalogo OpenRouter, poi ti propongo una mappatura concreta prima di riscrivere.

Recupero il catalogo attuale per le famiglie in uso (deepseek, qwen, kimi, gemini, gpt, grok, claude) con prezzi reali e data di rilascio.

input.command:
cd /home/eldios/dotfiles
f="$CLAUDE_JOB_DIR/tmp/models.json"
digest() {
  local pat="$1"
  jq -r --arg pat "$pat" '
    .data[]
    | select(.id | test($pat))
    | { id, created, ctx: .context_length,
        in: ((.pricing.prompt|tonumber)*1000000),
        out: ((.pricing.completion|tonumber)*1000000) }
    | "\(.id)\tin=$\(.in)/M out=$\(.out)/M ctx=\(.ctx)"
  ' "$f"
}
echo "=========== ANTHROPIC CLAUDE (opus/sonnet, current) ==========="
digest '^anthropic/claude' | sort
echo
echo "=========== OPENAI GPT ==========="
digest '^openai/gpt' | sort

content.text:
Ho il quadro completo dal catalogo OpenRouter attuale (luglio 2026, prezzi reali verificati). Ecco cosa propongo prima di riscrivere — i modelli attuali sono in gran parte superati e alcuni prezzi sono cambiati parecchio (l'opus è crollato da $15/$75 a $5/$25).

## Mappatura proposta (vecchio → nuovo, prezzi verificati $/M in·out)

| Ruolo | Ora | Nuovo | Prezzo | Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| self-hosted | ollama | *invariato* | — | litellm, non OpenRouter |
| cheap/fast (auto-suggest) | `deepseek-chat-v3-0324` | `deepseek/deepseek-v4-flash` | $0.09/$0.18 | 1M ctx, nuovo flagship cheap |
| reasoning cheap | `deepseek-r1-0528` | `deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro` | $0.435/$0.87 | 1M ctx |
| coder (default) | `qwen/qwen3-coder` | *invariato* | $0.22/$1.80 | ancora attuale, 1M ctx |
| kimi | `moonshotai/kimi-k2` | `moonshotai/kimi-k2-thinking` | $0.6/$2.5 | oppure `kimi-k2.7-code` $0.74/$3.5 |
| gemini flash | `gemini-2.5-flash` | `google/gemini-3.5-flash` | $1.5/$9 | o `gemini-3.1-flash-lite` $0.25/$1.5 |
| gemini pro (rag) | `gemini-2.5-pro` | `google/gemini-3.1-pro-preview` | $2/$12 | è *preview*; lo stabile resta 2.5-pro |
| gpt medio | `gpt-4.1` | `openai/gpt-5.4` | $2.5/$15 | 1M ctx |
| gpt flagship | `gpt-5` | `openai/gpt-5.5` | $5/$30 | 1M ctx |
| grok (dual_boost) | `x-ai/grok-4` *(rimosso da OR)* | `x-ai/grok-4.5` | $2/$6 | 500k ctx |
| claude sonnet (dual_boost) | `claude-sonnet-4` | `anthropic/claude-sonnet-5` | $2/$10 | 1M ctx |
| claude opus | `claude-opus-4` | `anthropic/claude-opus-4.8` | $5/$25 | 1M ctx, ora molto più economico |

## Ottimizzazione possibile

I 12 blocchi provider sono quasi identici (~11 righe l'uno, differiscono solo per `model` e `max_tokens`). Posso collassarli con un helper Lua:

```lua
local key = "cmd:cat <sops-path>"
local function or_model(model, max)
  return {
    __inherited_from = "openai",
    api_key_name = key,
    endpoint = "https://openrouter.ai/api/v1",
    model = model,
    extra_request_body = { timeout = 120000, max_completion_tokens = max, max_tokens = max },
  }
end
providers = {
  deepseekV4flash = or_model("deepseek/deepseek-v4-flash", 120000),
  ...
}
```

Passa da ~200 righe a ~30, stesso comportamento. I prezzi restano come commento sopra ogni entry.

Due decisioni prima di procedere:

questions.question: Rinomino le chiavi provider per rispecchiare i nuovi modelli (es. gemini25pro→gemini3pro, claude4sonnet→claudeSonnet5) aggiornando i 4 riferimenti interni, o le tengo stabili?

options.label: Rinomina
options.description: Chiavi coerenti coi modelli nuovi. Aggiorno i riferimenti: provider (main), auto_suggestions_provider, dual_boost first/second, rag.provider. Diff piu' ampio ma piu' pulito.

options.label: Tieni stabili
options.description: Cambio solo model= e prezzi sotto le chiavi esistenti. Le chiavi restano ma diventano fuorvianti (es. 'gemini25pro' punta a gemini-3.1). Diff minimo, zero rischio sui riferimenti.

questions.question: Applico l'helper Lua per collassare i 12 blocchi provider (~200 righe → ~30)?

options.label: Si, usa l'helper
options.description: Molto piu' compatto e manutenibile, comportamento identico. Verifico che il Lua sia valido.

options.label: No, blocchi espliciti
options.description: Mantengo un blocco per provider (verboso ma esplicito, ogni provider indipendente e ovvio da modificare).

result.output: Your questions have been answered: "Rinomino le chiavi provider per rispecchiare i nuovi modelli (es. gemini25pro→gemini3pro, claude4sonnet→claudeSonnet5) aggiornando i 4 riferimenti interni, o le tengo stabili?"="rendile generiche direi", "Applico l'helper Lua per collassare i 12 blocchi provider (~200 righe → ~30)?"="Si, usa l'helper". You can now continue with these answers in mind.

content.text: Ricevuto entrambi i messaggi: chiavi **generiche** (per ruolo/tier, non per modello), helper Lua **sì**, e trim dei provider inutili + aggiungere i migliori disponibili (es. GLM). Verifico GLM sul catalogo — non do niente per scontato sulla versione.

input.command:
cd /home/eldios/dotfiles
f="$CLAUDE_JOB_DIR/tmp/models.json"
digest() {
  jq -r --arg pat "$1" '
    .data[] | select(.id | test($pat))
    | "\(.id)\tin=$\(((.pricing.prompt|tonumber)*1000000))/M out=$\(((.pricing.completion|tonumber)*1000000))/M ctx=\(.context_length)"
  ' "$f" | sort
}
echo "=== GLM (z-ai / glm / zhipu) ==="; digest 'glm|zhipu|z-ai'
echo; echo "=== MINIMAX ==="; digest 'minimax'
echo; echo "=== any 'glm' anywhere in id ==="; jq -r '.data[].id' "$f" | grep -i glm
input.description: Find GLM and minimax models

content.text:
GLM 5.2 confermato: `z-ai/glm-5.2` a **$0.56/$1.76** per M, contesto 1M — ottimo rapporto, ed è l'ultimo GLM. Lo aggiungo.

Il parametro `max_tokens` viene inviato all'API, quindi se supera il max output del modello rischia un 400. Verifico i limiti reali di output per i modelli scelti invece di riusare i numeri arbitrari attuali.

input.command:
cd /home/eldios/dotfiles
f="$CLAUDE_JOB_DIR/tmp/models.json"
for id in "deepseek/deepseek-v4-flash" "deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro" "qwen/qwen3-coder" "z-ai/glm-5.2" "anthropic/claude-sonnet-5" "anthropic/claude-opus-4.8" "google/gemini-3.1-pro-preview" "x-ai/grok-4.5"; do
  jq -r --arg id "$id" '.data[] | select(.id==$id) | "\(.id)\tctx=\(.context_length)\tmax_out=\(.top_provider.max_completion_tokens // "null")"' "$f"
done
input.description: Check max output tokens per chosen model

content.text:
Ho tutto: GLM 5.2, prezzi reali, limiti di output verificati, e litellm/ollama da rimuovere. Roster finale (8 provider OpenRouter, chiavi generiche per ruolo, helper Lua):

`fast` deepseek-v4-flash · `coder` qwen3-coder · `reasoning` deepseek-v4-pro · `glm` glm-5.2 · `gemini` gemini-3.1-pro · `grok` grok-4.5 · `smart` claude-sonnet-5 · `pro` claude-opus-4.8.

Applico. Prima l'helper, poi i riferimenti, poi il blocco provider.

input.command:

content.text:
La sezione avante è pulita e coerente. Riepilogo.

## Cosa ho fatto sulla sezione avante

**Modelli aggiornati** (tutti verificati sul catalogo OpenRouter live, prezzi $/M in·out):

| Chiave (generica) | Modello nuovo | Prezzo | Ruolo |
|---|---|---|---|
| `fast` | deepseek-v4-flash | $0.09/$0.18 | auto-suggestions |
| `coder` | qwen3-coder | $0.22/$1.80 | **provider default** |
| `reasoning` | deepseek-v4-pro | $0.44/$0.87 | ragionamento |
| `glm` | **z-ai/glm-5.2** | $0.56/$1.76 | aggiunto (tuo richiesto) |
| `gemini` | gemini-3.1-pro-preview | $2/$12 | rag |
| `grok` | grok-4.5 | $2/$6 | dual_boost #2 |
| `smart` | claude-sonnet-5 | $2/$10 | dual_boost #1 |
| `pro` | claude-opus-4.8 | $5/$25 | premium |

**Rimossi** (non più utili/superati): `ollama`/litellm (dismesso), `deepseek-r1-0528`, `deepseek-chat-v3`, `kimi-k2`, `gemini-2.5-flash`, `gpt-4.1`, `gpt-5`, `grok-4` (ritirato da OpenRouter). Da 12 provider a 8.

**Ottimizzazione**: i 12 blocchi verbosi (~11 righe l'uno) collassati in un helper Lua `openrouter(model, max)` + 8 righe di definizioni. **−217/+34 righe**.

**Chiavi generiche** (per ruolo, senza versione baked-in) come richiesto; aggiornati tutti i 5 riferimenti interni (`provider`, `auto_suggestions_provider`, `dual_boost.first/second`, `rag.provider`).

**Dettaglio tecnico**: il parametro `max` è il cap di output-token, impostato entro il limite reale di ogni modello (verificato via `top_provider.max_completion_tokens`) per evitare 400 dall'API — non più i valori arbitrari tipo 512000 di prima.

## Verifiche fatte
- `nix-instantiate --parse`: **OK** (heredoc `''…''` intatto).
- **Lua di avante validato** con `luajit -b`: sintassi OK (il parse Nix da solo non lo controlla).
- Nessun riferimento residuo a chiavi/modelli vecchi; tutti i `provider =` risolvono a chiavi definite.
- Prezzi e ID modelli presi dalla fonte canonica (OpenRouter `/api/v1/models`), non a memoria.

## Da sapere
- Il secret `tokens/litellm/neovim/key` **resta in `sops.nix`**: è ancora usato da `waveterm-ai-providers.json` (3 ref). Neovim non lo usa più, ma non l'ho rimosso perché romperei waveterm. Dimmi se vuoi dismettere litellm anche lì.
- `gemini` punta a `gemini-3.1-pro-preview` (è *preview*); lo stabile sarebbe `gemini-2.5-pro`. Se preferisci stabilità per il rag, lo cambio.
- Default provider lasciato su `coder` (comportamento attuale). Con GLM 5.2 disponibile, se vuoi puoi metterlo default cambiando una parola.

Nessun commit fatto (come da tue regole): file modificato nel working tree, pronto per review. Non ho riformattato con alejandra (il file era già non-conforme prima, eviterei un diff estraneo).

Based on the search results, here's a comparison of the official Claude Code Neovim plugin options:

## Official vs. Community Implementations

coder/claudecode.nvim is the official Coder Neovim plugin for Claude Code with native nvim integration including terminal splits, file context injection, selection-aware prompts, and LSP integration. This plugin implements the same WebSocket-based MCP protocol used by Anthropic's official VS Code and JetBrains extensions.

When Anthropic released Claude Code with VS Code and JetBrains support, the project lead reverse-engineered their extension and built the Neovim plugin. This plugin implements the same WebSocket-based MCP protocol, giving Neovim users the same AI-powered coding experience.

## Comparison: coder/claudecode.nvim vs greggh/claude-code.nvim

**coder/claudecode.nvim (Official):**
- Pure Lua implementation
- Exposes 10 MCP tools that Claude can invoke to interact with the editor
- Built by the Coder team with deep protocol knowledge

**greggh/claude-code.nvim:**
- A lightweight Neovim plugin that opens Claude in a floating terminal and passes visual selections as context
- Features include toggling Claude Code with a key press, support for command-line arguments like --continue, and customizable window positions
- More terminal-focused approach

Both plugins work with the official Claude CLI, but the Coder plugin provides deeper editor integration through the MCP protocol.

## (5) Minimum Neovim Version
**Neovim >= 0.8.0**

## (6) Key Features vs Simple Terminal Toggle
Beyond basic toggling, this plugin provides:
- Real-time file/selection context synchronization with Claude
- Native diff viewing with accept/reject workflow
- WebSocket MCP protocol implementation enabling Claude to interact with your editor
- Multi-file context sending from file explorers
- Model selection interface

## (7) Claude CLI Requirement

content.text:
Ho verificato. Ecco il quadro reale (luglio 2026):

## Non esiste un plugin Neovim *first-party* di Anthropic
Anthropic pubblica estensioni ufficiali solo per **VS Code** e **JetBrains**. Per Neovim il de-facto standard è **`coder/claudecode.nvim`** (del team Coder): non è di Anthropic, ma implementa **lo stesso protocollo WebSocket/MCP** delle estensioni ufficiali — quindi è "ufficiale a livello di protocollo" ed è molto più sviluppato del `greggh/claude-code.nvim` che usi ora.

## Confronto

| | `greggh/claude-code.nvim` (attuale) | `coder/claudecode.nvim` |
|---|---|---|
| Cosa fa | apre la CLI `claude` in un terminale float e passa la selezione | integrazione editor via protocollo MCP |
| Diff inline accept/reject | ❌ | ✅ nativo |
| Contesto file/selezione in tempo reale | parziale | ✅ (10 tool MCP che Claude può invocare) |
| Model select, invio da file explorer | ❌ | ✅ |
| Manutenzione | terminale-centrico, meno attivo | **2.9k star, v0.3.0 (set 2025), attivo** |
| Requisiti | CLI `claude` | CLI `claude`, Neovim ≥0.8, dep `folke/snacks.nvim` |

La dipendenza `folke/snacks.nvim` **è già inclusa in LazyVim** (fa parte del core), quindi per te non aggiunge nulla di nuovo.

## Proposta di migrazione
Sostituire il blocco `xdg.configFile."nvim/lua/plugins/claude-code.lua"` attuale con la spec di `coder/claudecode.nvim`, mantenendo `<leader>ac` come toggle (la tua muscle-memory) e aggiungendo i keymap per diff accept/deny/resume/continue/model-select.

Una nota di attenzione: sia questo plugin sia **avante** che l'attuale claude-code usano il prefisso `<leader>a*` — LazyVim mappa `<leader>a` come gruppo "+ai". Non è un problema nuovo (ce l'hai già), ma alla migrazione controllo che i tasti scelti non collidano con avante.

Procedo con lo swap?

options.label: Lascia greggh
options.description: Resto sul plugin attuale, magari solo aggiornando la config esistente.

## Key findings on your specific questions

**1. blink.cmp** — Yes, it is now the de-facto modern completion engine. LazyVim ships `blink.cmp` as its **default** completion (via the `coding.blink` extra); `nvim-cmp` is now the opt-in alternative extra, and exactly one is active at a time. Latest stable v1.10.2 (2026-04). No notable stability issues surfaced; recent releases are bugfix/polish. **KEEP.**

**3. dressing.nvim** — Confirmed **ARCHIVED**. README top notice, verbatim:
> "This plugin is archived! It still works, but I recommend that you use snacks.nvim instead for your `vim.ui.*` interfaces."
Since you almost certainly already have snacks.nvim (LazyVim core), just enable `snacks.input`/`snacks.select` and drop dressing. **REPLACE.**

**4. render-markdown vs markview** — Both are actively developed in 2026. render-markdown.nvim (v8.13.0, 2026-06-18) is the one broadly wired into AI-chat plugins (avante, codecompanion, copilot-chat) and is what LazyVim's AI extras pull in. markview.nvim (v28.3.0, 2026-05-16) is equally maintained but a heavier, more feature-broad renderer. **No reason to switch off render-markdown.**

**6. nvim-cmp redundancy (important)** — nvim-cmp is not archived and still maintained, but if blink.cmp is your main engine, having nvim-cmp loaded **only** as an avante dependency is redundant. avante can use blink directly via **Kaiser-Yang/blink-cmp-avante** (add it as a blink dependency and add `avante` to blink sources), or via `blink.compat`. Recommendation: switch avante to the blink-cmp-avante provider and drop the nvim-cmp dependency, so you don't run two completion frameworks. **REMOVE-redundant.**

**7. mini.pick / nvim-web-devicons** — Both current and fine. Note the org rename: mini.pick now lives under `nvim-mini/mini.nvim` (was `echasnovski`). The only "modern shift" worth knowing: LazyVim's default picker is now **snacks.picker**, and **mini.icons** is a lighter icon provider many configs adopt — but neither is a required migration; your current choices are fully supported.

Sources: GitHub API/releases for each repo (archived, pushed_at, stargazers, latest tag as cited above); [dressing README](https://github.com/stevearc/dressing.nvim); [LazyVim Blink extra](http://www.lazyvim.org/extras/coding/blink); [avante blink support issue #959](https://github.com/yetone/avante.nvim/issues/959) and [blink-cmp-avante](https://github.com/Kaiser-Yang/blink-cmp-avante).</result>

Fix commento OK (parse OK). Ecco l'audit completo, tutto verificato sui repo reali (8-9 luglio 2026).

## Verdetto per plugin

| Plugin | Stato | Azione |
|---|---|---|
| `blink.cmp` | attivo, default LazyVim | **tieni** |
| `mcphub.nvim` | standard MCP, attivo (cadenza rallentata) | **tieni** |
| `render-markdown.nvim` | attivo, integrato con i plugin AI | **tieni** |
| `img-clip.nvim`, `mini.pick`, `web-devicons` | ok | **tieni** |
| `dressing.nvim` | **ARCHIVIATO** (README rimanda a snacks) | **sostituisci** con `snacks.input` |
| `nvim-cmp` | ridondante (solo dep di avante, tu usi blink) | **rimuovi** → `blink-cmp-avante` |
| `claude-code` | ✅ già migrato a coder/claudecode.nvim | fatto |

## Il punto vero: avante

- **Non esiste** un plugin Neovim first-party di Anthropic (confermato).
- `avante.nvim` **non è morto**: attivo, ora con 2 maintainer, ha aggiunto ACP. Ma: build **Rust** (`avante_lib`, `make`) — attrito su NixOS (tu infatti lo installi via nixpkgs + gcc/gnumake/binutils apposta) — e storia di breaking changes. 18k star.
- L'alternativa forte è **`olimorris/codecompanion.nvim`**: **puro Lua, zero build**, adapter OpenRouter nativo, integrazione mcphub, ACP (può pilotare Claude Code/Gemini/Codex dentro la chat), release disciplinate, ottima reputazione di stabilità. 6.7k star, ma **maintainer singolo** (bus-factor).
- `claudecode.nvim` **non** sostituisce avante: è solo-Claude, niente OpenRouter multi-modello.

**Raccomandazione dell'analisi** (confidenza ~70%, è una scelta di preferenza non di correttezza): per il ruolo "chat multi-provider + OpenRouter + mcphub", valutare la migrazione avante → codecompanion — su NixOS elimina l'attrito del build Rust e mantiene intatto il tuo setup OpenRouter/mcphub. Non è che avante sia rotto: se ti piace la UX Cursor-style inline di avante, è il motivo per restare.

Nota: `minuet-ai.nvim` è ortogonale (ghost-text as-you-type, che né avante né codecompanion fanno bene) — posso aggiungerlo a parte se lo vuoi.

Cosa faccio con avante?

input.command:
cd /home/eldios/dotfiles
F=common/home-manager/eldios/programs/neovim.nix
echo "=== nix parse ==="
nix-instantiate --parse "$F" >/dev/null && echo "PARSE_OK" || echo "PARSE_FAIL"
check_lua() {
  local name="$1"; local out="$CLAUDE_JOB_DIR/tmp/$2"
  awk -v pat="$name" '
    $0 ~ ("xdg\\.configFile\\.\"nvim/lua/plugins/" pat "\"\\.text = '"'"''"'"'") {grab=1; next}
    grab && /^  '"'"''"'"';/ {grab=0}
    grab {print}
  ' "$F" > "$out"
  perl -0pi -e 's/\$\{[^}]*\}/STUB/g' "$out"
  if nix-shell -p luajit --run "luajit -bl '$out' /dev/null" >/dev/null 2>"$CLAUDE_JOB_DIR/tmp/e"; then
    echo "LUA_OK: $2 ($(wc -l < "$out") lines)"
  else
    echo "LUA_FAIL: $2"; cat "$CLAUDE_JOB_DIR/tmp/e"
  fi
}
check_lua 'blink-cmp\.lua' blink.lua
check_lua 'avante\.lua' avante.lua

content.text:
Capito il ragionamento, e ha senso. Sulla tua memoria: quando eri passato ad avante, codecompanion era più scarno (avante aveva chat, multi-history ecc.). Oggi (dall'audit di luglio 2026) codecompanion ha una chat buffer matura e la cronologia multi-sessione esiste via estensione — quindi il gap si è ridotto — ma non ho verificato feature per feature la parità con la UX di avante, e comunque **la tua decisione è chiara: teniamo avante**. Se un giorno vorrai ricontrollare, resta valida l'opzione "provarli affiancati" e a quel punto verifico nel dettaglio chat/history/UX prima di qualsiasi migrazione. Non riapro la scelta ora.

## Pulizia dipendenze avante — fatta e verificata

- **`dressing.nvim` rimosso** (archiviato). `vim.ui.select/input` passa a snacks (core LazyVim). Se input/select ti apparissero "spogli", basta abilitare `snacks.input` — di default in LazyVim è già attivo.
- **`nvim-cmp` rimosso**: non giri più due framework di completion. Il completamento di mention/comandi di avante ora arriva da **`blink-cmp-avante`**, aggiunto come source di blink (solo `"avante"` in `sources.default`, appeso via `opts_extend` di LazyVim così restano lsp/path/snippets).
- Rimosse anche le dep opzionali **commentate** (telescope, fzf-lua, copilot) — codice morto, coerente con la tua regola.

**Verifica:** `nix-instantiate --parse` OK; sintassi Lua di `blink-cmp.lua` e `avante.lua` validata con `luajit -b`; nessun riferimento residuo a `nvim-cmp`/`dressing`.

## Nota (regola commenti)
In `blink-cmp.lua` c'è un commento **pre-esistente** fuorviante: `-- Disable cmdline completion entirely...` ma sotto `cmdline.enabled = true` (quindi NON è disabilitato). È il tipo di commento-che-mente che ti dà fastidio. Non l'ho toccato perché fuori dallo scope di questo giro — dimmi se lo sistemo.

## Riepilogo sessione su `neovim.nix` (nel working tree, **non committato**)
Complessivo **+69 / −255** righe. In ordine: pulizia iniziale (cruft/bug), refresh modelli+prezzi avante con helper Lua (+GLM 5.2, −litellm), swap Claude Code → `coder/claudecode.nvim`, pulizia dipendenze avante. Non ho riformattato con alejandra (il file era già non-conforme prima). Pronto per la tua review e firma.

result: neovim.nix — audit plugin completato (fonti verificate lug 2026); Claude Code migrato a coder/claudecode.nvim, avante mantenuto con dipendenze ripulite (dressing→snacks, nvim-cmp→blink-cmp-avante, dep morte rimosse); parse Nix + sintassi Lua validati, non committato.
leggi su reddit ed in giro dei confronti e cosa dice la gente nel 2026 .. apri pure un agent che fa un confronto tra i due

Apro un agente dedicato che spulcia Reddit (r/neovim), HN, blog, discussioni GitHub e video del 2026 per raccogliere il sentiment reale della gente su avante vs codecompanion — con citazioni e URL, non impressioni.

input.description: Community comparison avante vs codecompanion
input.prompt:
Research what the Neovim community actually says in 2026 comparing the two AI-assistant plugins **yetone/avante.nvim** vs **olimorris/codecompanion.nvim**. This is qualitative sentiment research — I want real user opinions, not the plugins' own docs.

Sources to mine (use WebSearch + WebFetch, cite exact URLs):
- Reddit, especially r/neovim (search "avante codecompanion", "codecompanion vs avante", "best neovim AI plugin 2026", "avante alternative")
- Hacker News threads
- Blog posts / dotfiles writeups from 2025-2026
- GitHub Discussions/issues where users compare them
- YouTube video descriptions/comments if surfaced

For EACH plugin, capture what users actually report in 2026:
- Strengths people praise (UX, chat buffer, inline edit, multi-provider, agentic/tools, MCP, ACP)
- Complaints/pain points (stability, breaking changes, build friction, bugs, config complexity)
- Which one people migrated TO and FROM, and WHY (direction of movement matters)
- Sentiment on the specific features my user cares about: **chat UI quality, multi-session chat history, and overall UX polish** — my user prefers avante's UX and recalls codecompanion historically lacked chat/multi-history; check whether 2026 opinions confirm or contradict that codecompanion has caught up.

Important constraints:
- VERIFY and CITE. Every claim tied to a source URL + rough date. Prefer 2026 (and late-2025) posts; flag when something is older and may be stale.
- Quote 1-2 representative user comments per plugin where possible (short quotes).
- Distinguish "loud minority" from broad consensus if you can tell.
- Note recency: both plugins move fast, so a 2024 opinion may no longer hold.

Deliver:
1. A short sentiment summary per plugin (what the community consensus is in 2026).
2. A "who should pick which" synthesis.
3. Specifically address the chat/multi-history/UX-polish question with evidence.
4. A sources list (URLs with dates).
Keep it tight and evidence-driven. Do not recommend changing my config — just report what people say.

input.description: Claude Code agent management docs
input.prompt:
The user runs Claude Code as a CLI inside Neovim (via the coder/claudecode.nvim plugin, which launches the `claude` CLI in a terminal split). They ask how to "manage agents" in Claude Code. Give an authoritative, CURRENT (2026) answer — verify against official docs / `claude` CLI help rather than memory, and cite where each fact comes from.

Cover concretely:
1. Subagents in Claude Code: what they are, how automatic delegation works, and how to invoke one explicitly.
2. Creating/managing custom agents: the `/agents` slash command (what it lets you do — create, edit, list, set tools/model), and the `.claude/agents/*.md` file format — exact frontmatter fields (name, description, tools, model, etc.) and precedence (project `.claude/agents/` vs user `~/.claude/agents/`).
3. Running/observing agents: how subagents run (parallel, isolated context), any background-agent / task management, and slash commands to inspect them.
4. How ALL of this works when Claude Code runs inside a Neovim terminal (coder/claudecode.nvim): confirm that agent management is a property of the `claude` CLI session itself, so the `/agents` command and `.claude/agents/` files work identically whether launched from a shell or from the Neovim terminal — the plugin doesn't add or change agent management, it just hosts the CLI. Note anything neovim-specific if it exists.
5. Briefly: how is this DIFFERENT from an MCP server (mcphub.nvim) and from a plugin's own agentic mode? Just clarify the boundary so the user isn't confused.

Keep it practical and command-oriented (what to type, what file to create). Cite official doc URLs. If a detail can't be verified, say so rather than inventing it.

content.text:
Intanto l'inquadramento (i dettagli operativi esatti dei comandi CLI te li do quando torna l'agente):

## Sono due mondi distinti

**1. Agenti di Claude Code** — vivono nella sessione `claude` che `claudecode.nvim` apre nel terminale dentro neovim. `claudecode.nvim` è solo un *host* del terminale: non aggiunge né cambia la gestione agenti, quindi li gestisci **identico a come faresti da shell**.
- **Subagent**: task isolati che Claude lancia in parallelo con contesto separato (es. proprio adesso ne ho due in background — il confronto avante/codecompanion e uno per questa domanda).
- **Custom agent**: file `.claude/agents/*.md` (per-progetto) o `~/.claude/agents/*.md` (globale), con frontmatter (name, description, tools, model). Si creano/gestiscono col comando `/agents`.
- In pratica dentro neovim: apri la chat Claude (`<leader>ai` col nuovo plugin), e lì digiti `/agents`, oppure crei i file `.md` — funziona uguale.

**2. Agenti "AI" nativi dei plugin neovim** — NON sono Claude Code, sono un layer diverso:
- **avante**: modalità agentic/tool-use + supporto **ACP** (Agent Client Protocol) per pilotare agenti esterni.
- **mcphub**: **non** è un agent — gestisce i **server MCP** (tool/risorse) che gli agenti possono richiamare. Il tuo `mpc-hub.lua` configura questo.

La confusione tipica è tra questi due: Claude Code (subagent + `.claude/agents/`) è una cosa; gli "agenti" di avante/mcphub dentro neovim sono un'altra. `claudecode.nvim` ti fa usare i primi dentro l'editor; avante/mcphub sono i secondi.

Ti mando i comandi/formati esatti (verificati sui doc) appena l'agente `claude-code-guide` finisce. Ho anche l'altro agente in corso sul confronto community avante vs codecompanion.

# avante.nvim vs codecompanion.nvim — Neovim community sentiment, 2026

## Method note
Most "2026" writeups are blog/aggregator pieces that recycle the same GitHub Discussion (`codecompanion.nvim` #1209). The richest primary sentiment is in that discussion, the LazyVim discussion, Lobsters/HN threads, and the history-extension repo. Reddit r/neovim threads exist but were not directly reachable via the search index today (US-only WebSearch surfaced blog re-syntheses of them rather than the threads themselves) — flagged where relevant. I also pulled live repo stats to sanity-check the "loud" claims (some are stale).

## Live repo stats (verified 2026-07-08/09 via GitHub API)
- **avante.nvim**: 18,028 stars, ~36 open issues+PRs, last push 2026-07-08 — actively maintained.
- **codecompanion.nvim**: 6,727 stars, ~9 open issues+PRs, last push 2026-07-08 — actively maintained.
- **codecompanion-history.nvim** (the history extension): created 2025-05-03, 188 stars, last push 2026-01-22.

Note: older comparisons (Feb 2025) cite "203 open issues for Avante vs 1 for CodeCompanion." That figure is **stale** — avante now sits at ~36. Use the issue-count argument with caution; it aged.

---

## 1. Sentiment summary per plugin

### avante.nvim — "Cursor inside Neovim"
**Praised for:** the diff-first sidebar workflow (select → describe → review diff → accept/reject with one keystroke), broad multi-provider support (Claude, OpenAI, Gemini, Copilot, Ollama, plus ACP agents: claude-code, gemini-cli, codex, goose, kimi-cli), per-project `avante.md` steering (the CLAUDE.md parallel), and well-handled code diffs. Users doing non-trivial refactors specifically prefer it because the explicit review step matters (Samuel Lawrentz blog, 2026-03-26).

**Complained about (recurring, from users who left):**
- "too loaded, heavy and hard to use now. There is just no documentation" — *pratyushmittal*, CC Discussion #1209.
- "buggy and it doesn't work with copilot," plus unresolved bug reports — *TheLazyCat00*, #1209.
- Context overflow causing freezing, slow issue resolution, confusing release notes, and repeated build-artifact re-downloads on update — *gitsang*, #1209.
- Build friction: `make BUILD_FROM_SOURCE=true` trips up new users (avante GitHub issues).
- Breaking changes tracking fast-moving model APIs (e.g. the Opus 4.7 `temperature` deprecation 400-error, issue #3022).
- Keybinding conflicts with LazyVim defaults (LazyVim Discussion #4402).

**Consensus read:** avante is the popular, feature-rich, higher-velocity option — and that velocity is exactly the double-edged sword. The core knock is *internal complexity / instability / thin docs*, not the UX. Its sidebar+diff UX is consistently the thing people *praise*, even detractors.

### codecompanion.nvim — "feels like Neovim, not Cursor"
**Praised for:** documentation quality and simplicity ("love CodeCompanion for its documentation and simplicity" — *pratyushmittal*), a clean codebase that's easy to modify/extend, clear release notes flagging breaking changes, deep native integration (chat buffers in splits, `@lsp`/`@buffers` variables, slash commands, prompt library, GitHub Models free tier), and a cautious maintainer who ships fewer but more stable features.

**Complained about:**
- Cautious pace means "might take longer to solve problems" (*gitsang*, #1209).
- Historically weaker at *agentic* coding: "works well for interactive chat but falls short for agentic coding… limited to pre-configured 'workflows'" (HN #44839311, ~2025).
- Chat persistence historically clunky: "CodeCompanionSave/CodeCompanionLoad requires quite the dance" (Discussion #139/#652).

**Consensus read:** the "Neovim-native, stable, well-documented, extensible" choice. The recurring reservation is agentic depth and (historically) chat persistence — both of which have been actively closing in late-2025/2026.

---

## 2. Who should pick which (community synthesis)

- **Pick avante** if you want a Cursor-like, diff-first review workflow for non-trivial refactors, the widest provider/ACP-agent menu out of the box, and per-project `avante.md` steering — and you can tolerate faster churn, occasional bugs, thinner docs, and a source build.
- **Pick codecompanion** if you value Neovim-native feel, stability, excellent docs, an easily-hackable/extensible codebase, and tight LSP/buffer context — and you're doing more chat/in-flow work than heavy autonomous agentic edits.
- **The most-repeated actual advice: install both.** They don't conflict; bind to different keymaps and use avante for big diffs, codecompanion for in-flow questions (Samuel Lawrentz 2026; *gitsang* #1209). Several heavy users also run an external CLI (Claude Code in a tmux pane) for truly agentic work regardless of plugin (HN #44839311, Lobsters).

**Direction of migration:** the visible movement in the primary sources is **Avante → CodeCompanion** (pratyushmittal, TheLazyCat00, caesar-ipl in #1209), driven by avante's complexity/bugs/doc gaps — not by feature envy. I found **no** documented CodeCompanion → Avante migrations, though this is partly a sampling artifact (the richest thread lives on the CodeCompanion repo, so it over-samples people who chose CC). Treat "everyone is leaving avante" as a **loud, self-selected minority**, not proven broad consensus: avante still has ~2.7x the stars and heavy ongoing use.

---

## 3. The chat UI / multi-session history / UX-polish question (your specific concern)

Your recollection — *you prefer avante's UX; codecompanion historically lacked chat + multi-history* — was **accurate for 2024–early 2025, but is now contradicted by 2026 reality on the history point.**

- **Chat multi-session history — codecompanion has caught up, and arguably surpassed avante here.**
  - Native: CodeCompanion now supports **ACP session resume** — `/resume` in a fresh chat buffer calls `session/list` → `session/load` to restore prior conversations across restarts (docs last updated 2026-06-30; ACP Discussion #2313).
  - Extension: **`ravitemer/codecompanion-history.nvim`** (created 2025-05-03, 188 stars) gives full multi-session management — save/browse/restore, smart auto-generated titles, project-aware filtering, chat duplication, and a vector-search summary system over past chats (telescope/snacks/fzf-lua pickers). This is genuinely richer multi-history than avante ships. It preserves messages, tools, schemas, and references.
  - Caveat: this is a *third-party extension*, not core, and its last push was 2026-01-22 (stable but less active); there's a known open bug (issue #63) about message history not passed to ACP adapters on restore. Avante's own persisted, browsable multi-session history is comparatively thin — avante is centered on the live sidebar/diff loop, not a chat archive.

- **Chat UI quality / conversations — codecompanion is strong; this is arguably its home turf.** Its chat-buffer-in-a-split model, slash commands, `@` context variables, and prompt library are consistently what people praise (#1209, Samuel Lawrentz). TheLazyCat00's reason for leaving avante was literally that CC "supports conversations (not just buffer editing)." So the framing "codecompanion lacked chat" is now inverted — chat is its strength.

- **Overall UX polish — this is where your preference still holds up.** "Polish" splits two ways in the sources:
  - *Interaction polish for edits*: avante's one-keystroke diff accept/reject sidebar is repeatedly called the more Cursor-like, satisfying flow for applying changes. If "UX polish" means the inline-edit/diff experience, avante still wins in community sentiment.
  - *Docs / stability / coherence polish*: codecompanion is judged the more polished, less janky, better-documented product. Avante's UX is praised but its *reliability and docs* are the top complaint.

**Bottom line for you:** the specific gap you remember (no chat, no multi-history in codecompanion) has been closed as of 2026 — via native ACP resume plus `codecompanion-history.nvim`. Your preference for avante's *diff/sidebar interaction feel* is still shared by the community; but if the deciding factor is "multi-session chat history + chat UI," codecompanion is now at least on par and by several accounts ahead — with the asterisk that its best history story depends on a third-party extension.

---

## 4. Sources (with dates / recency flags)

- **CodeCompanion vs Avante — Discussion #1209**, olimorris/codecompanion.nvim — richest primary sentiment (Avante→CC migrations, complaints). https://github.com/olimorris/codecompanion.nvim/discussions/1209 *(2025, ongoing)*
- **Samuel Lawrentz, "avante.nvim vs CodeCompanion — AI Inside Neovim in 2026"** — 2026-03-26. https://samuellawrentz.com/blog/neovim-ai-plugins-avante-codecompanion/ *(current; opinion + framing)*
- **ravitemer/codecompanion-history.nvim** — multi-session history extension. https://github.com/ravitemer/codecompanion-history.nvim *(created 2025-05-03, 188★, last push 2026-01-22)*
- **CodeCompanion ACP / session resume docs** — https://codecompanion.olimorris.dev/agent-client-protocol and /usage/acp-protocol *(docs updated 2026-06-30)*; ACP session mgmt Discussion #2313.
- **"Save and restore chats" #139 / "Working Chat History Extension" #652** — historical evidence CC persistence was clunky. https://github.com/olimorris/codecompanion.nvim/discussions/139 *(older, 2024–2025 — the "dance" complaint is now partly addressed)*
- **HN #44839311** — "agentic stuff with neovim" — CC "falls short for agentic coding"; people fall back to Claude CLI. https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=44839311 *(~2025, ~11 months old)*
- **HN #41353835** — original avante Show HN. https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=41353835 *(2024 — stale, launch-era)*

È tornato l'agente sul confronto community 2026. Sintesi (fonti verificate, stat repo dal vivo l'8-9 lug 2026):

## avante vs codecompanion — cosa dice la gente nel 2026

**Stato:** entrambi attivissimi. avante **18k star** (~36 issue aperte), codecompanion **6.7k** (~9). Nota: il vecchio dato "203 vs 1 issue" che gira nei blog è **stale** — avante ora è a ~36.

**avante — "Cursor dentro Neovim"**
- *Lodato*: workflow sidebar diff-first (seleziona → descrivi → rivedi diff → accetta/rifiuta con un tasto), menu provider più ampio (+ agenti ACP), steering per-progetto `avante.md`. **Anche chi lo lascia loda la UX.**
- *Criticato*: complessità/pesantezza, doc scarne, bug ("buggy, non va con copilot"), build friction (`make`), churn veloce che rompe cose, conflitti keymap con LazyVim.

**codecompanion — "sa di Neovim, non di Cursor"**
- *Lodato*: documentazione e semplicità, codebase pulita e hackabile, release note chiare sui breaking, chat buffer nativa con `@lsp`/`@buffers`, maintainer prudente = più stabile.
- *Criticato*: pace cauta ("ci mette di più a risolvere"), storicamente più debole sull'agentic e persistenza chat "macchinosa" — **entrambi in gran parte risolti nel 2025-2026.**

**Direzione delle migrazioni:** quella visibile è **avante → codecompanion** (complessità/bug/doc), MA è una minoranza rumorosa e auto-selezionata (il thread principale vive sul repo di codecompanion). avante ha ancora ~2.7x le star e sviluppo pieno. Consiglio più ripetuto: **tienili entrambi** (keymap diversi, avante per i diff grossi, codecompanion per le domande in-flow).

## La tua domanda specifica (chat / multi-history / UX)
Il tuo ricordo era **giusto per il 2024–inizio 2025, ma ora superato sulla history**:
- **Multi-history chat**: codecompanion ha recuperato e probabilmente **superato** avante — `/resume` nativo via ACP (session/list+load, doc agg. 30 giu 2026) + estensione **`ravitemer/codecompanion-history.nvim`** (save/browse/restore, titoli auto, ricerca vettoriale sulle chat passate). Più ricca di quel che offre avante. *Asterisco*: la parte migliore è un'**estensione terza**, non core.
- **Chat UI**: ora è il **punto di forza** di codecompanion, non più il buco.
- **UX polish**: qui la tua preferenza **regge ancora** — il flusso diff/sidebar one-keystroke di avante è quello che la community indica come più soddisfacente per applicare modifiche. avante vince sull'*interazione di edit*; codecompanion vince su *doc/stabilità/coerenza*.

**In sintesi:** la tua scelta di tenere avante è coerente con il sentiment se ciò che ami è il flusso diff/inline-edit. Il gap "codecompanion non ha chat/history" però nel 2026 non esiste più — se un domani il fattore decisivo diventasse l'archivio chat multi-sessione, codecompanion oggi è alla pari o avanti.

Fonti principali: [Discussion #1209](https://github.com/olimorris/codecompanion.nvim/discussions/1209), [Lawrentz 2026](https://samuellawrentz.com/blog/neovim-ai-plugins-avante-codecompanion/), [codecompanion-history.nvim](https://github.com/ravitemer/codecompanion-history.nvim), [ACP docs](https://codecompanion.olimorris.dev/agent-client-protocol), [HN #44839311](https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=44839311).

<result>Based on the official documentation I've fetched, here's a complete, current (2026) guide to managing agents in Claude Code:

## 1. Subagents: Automatic Delegation & Core Concepts

**What they are:** Subagents are specialized AI assistants that handle specific tasks in isolation, each running in its own context window with custom system prompts, tool restrictions, and permissions. When Claude encounters a task that matches a subagent's description, it automatically delegates to that subagent—the subagent works independently and returns only a summary to your main conversation.

**Why they exist:** Subagents preserve context by keeping exploration output, logs, or verbose results out of your main conversation. They enforce tool restrictions (e.g., read-only agents) and reduce costs by routing tasks to cheaper models like Haiku.

**Built-in subagents** (v2.1.198+, per https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents.md):
- **Explore**: Fast, read-only agent for file discovery and codebase exploration. Inherits the parent session's model (capped at Opus on Claude API).
- **Plan**: Read-only agent for research before presenting a plan. Inherits the parent session's model.
- **General-purpose**: Capable agent for multi-step tasks requiring both exploration and action. All tools, inherits the parent model.
- **Other helpers**: `statusline-setup` (Sonnet), `claude-code-guide` (Haiku)

---

## 2. Creating & Managing Custom Agents

**File Format and Location:**  
Subagent files are YAML-frontmatter Markdown files stored in these locations (highest to lowest priority):
1. Managed settings (organization-wide)
2. `--agents` CLI flag (current session only)
3. `.claude/agents/` (project scope)
4. `~/.claude/agents/` (user scope, all projects)
5. Plugin `agents/` directories (where plugin is enabled)

**Supported Frontmatter Fields** (from https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents.md#supported-frontmatter-fields):

| Field | Required | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `name` | Yes | Unique identifier (lowercase + hyphens). Used by hooks as `agent_type`. |
| `description` | Yes | When Claude should delegate to this agent. Be specific so Claude knows when to use it. |
| `tools` | No | Comma-separated list of allowed tools. If omitted, inherits all. |
| `disallowedTools` | No | Tools to explicitly deny. Applied before `tools`. Supports patterns like `mcp__<server>`. |
| `model` | No | `sonnet`, `opus`, `haiku`, `fable`, or full model ID (e.g. `claude-opus-4-8`), or `inherit`. Defaults to `inherit`. |
| `permissionMode` | No | `default`, `acceptEdits`, `auto`, `dontAsk`, `bypassPermissions`, `plan`, or `manual`. |
| `maxTurns` | No | Maximum agentic turns before the subagent stops. |
| `skills` | No | List of skills to preload into the subagent's context. Full skill content is injected. |
| `mcpServers` | No | MCP servers scoped to this subagent. Inline definitions or references to configured servers. |
| `hooks` | No | Lifecycle hooks (PreToolUse, PostToolUse, Stop). Ignored for plugin subagents. |
| `memory` | No | `user`, `project`, or `local`. Enables persistent cross-session learning. |
| `background` | No | `true` to always run in background. Default (v2.1.198+): runs in background by default. |
| `effort` | No | Effort level: `low`, `medium`, `high`, `xhigh`, `max`. Overrides session setting. |
| `isolation` | No | Set to `worktree` to run in an isolated git worktree. |
| `color` | No | Display color: `red`, `blue`, `green`, `yellow`, `purple`, `orange`, `pink`, or `cyan`. |
| `initialPrompt` | No | Auto-submitted as the first turn when agent runs as the main session (via `--agent` or `agent` setting). |

**Example file** (`.claude/agents/code-reviewer.md`):
```markdown
---
name: code-reviewer
description: Expert code review specialist. Proactively reviews code for quality, security, and maintainability. Use immediately after writing or modifying code.
tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash
model: sonnet
---

You are a senior code reviewer ensuring high standards of code quality and security.

When invoked:
1. Run git diff to see recent changes
2. Focus on modified files
3. Review for clarity, security, proper error handling, and test coverage

Provide feedback organized by priority:
- Critical issues (must fix)
- Warnings (should fix)
- Suggestions (consider improving)
```

**Creating subagents:**
- **Ask Claude to write it** (preferred): `Create a code-reviewer subagent in ~/.claude/agents/`
- **Write the file manually** in `.claude/agents/<name>.md` or `~/.claude/agents/<name>.md`
- **CLI flag** (session-only): `claude --agents '{"code-reviewer": {"description": "...", "tools": [...], "model": "sonnet", "prompt": "..."}}'`

---

## 3. Running & Observing Agents

**The `/agents` command** (v2.1.198+, per https://code.claude.com/docs/en/agents.md):  
Running `/agents` no longer opens an interactive wizard. Instead, it prints a reminder to ask Claude or edit `.claude/agents/` directly. Subagent files are watched automatically: changes within ~3 seconds are detected without restarting the session (caveat: after creating a new agents directory for the first time, a restart is required).

**Explicit invocation patterns** (from https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents.md#invoke-subagents-explicitly):
1. **Natural language**: name the subagent in your prompt (Claude decides whether to delegate)
   ```
   Use the code-reviewer subagent to look at my auth changes
   ```

2. **@-mention** (guarantees invocation): type `@` and select from typeahead
   ```
   @"code-reviewer (agent)" review this auth module
   ```
   Or manually: `@agent-code-reviewer`

3. **Session-wide default** (entire session uses that subagent's system prompt, tools, model):
   ```bash
   claude --agent code-reviewer
   ```
   Or in `.claude/settings.json`:
   ```json
   { "agent": "code-reviewer" }
   ```

**Running in foreground vs. background** (v2.1.198+):  
Subagents run in the **background by default**. Claude runs foreground when it needs the result immediately. You can steer this:
- Ask Claude explicitly: "run this task in the background"
- Press **Ctrl+B** to background a running task
- Set `background: true` in the subagent's frontmatter to force background
- Disable all background tasks: `CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_BACKGROUND_TASKS=1`

**Observing running work:**
- **Main session subagents**: Named background subagents appear in the @-mention typeahead with status
- **Background sessions** (different approach): `claude agents` opens agent view — a dashboard of all running sessions
- **Current session tasks**: `/tasks` lists each item and lets you check on, attach to, or stop them
- **Transcripts**: Each subagent creates a transcript file at `~/.claude/projects/{projectId}/{sessionId}/subagents/agent-{agentId}.jsonl`

**Resuming subagents**: Each subagent invocation creates a fresh instance. To continue an existing subagent's work:
```
Continue that code review and now analyze the authorization logic
```
Claude uses `SendMessage` with the agent ID or name to resume it, preserving full conversation history. (Built-in Explore and Plan agents are one-shot and can't be resumed; use `general-purpose` or a custom subagent when resumption is needed.)

---

## 4. Neovim-Specific: How Agents Work in the Coder/claudecode.nvim Plugin

**The short answer:** Agent management is a **property of the `claude` CLI session itself**, not the Neovim plugin. When Claude Code runs inside Neovim via **coder/claudecode.nvim**, the plugin simply hosts the `claude` CLI in a Neovim terminal split. All agent features — `/agents` command, `.claude/agents/` file watching, `--agent` flag, `--agents` JSON, @-mentions, foreground/background task switching (Ctrl+B), and agent invocation patterns — work **identically** whether launched from a shell or Neovim.

**Why:** The coder/claudecode.nvim plugin doesn't modify Claude Code's agent system or intercept agent management commands. It's a terminal wrapper that:
- Spawns the `claude` CLI in a Neovim terminal split
- Routes your keyboard input to the CLI's stdin
- Displays the CLI's stdout/stderr in the terminal

Since agents are managed by the CLI session, not the plugin, the `/agents` command, @-mentions for subagent selection, background task panels, and all agent control work exactly as documented.

**Caveat:** The plugin does **not** expose Neovim-specific agent discovery or UI. You cannot browse `.claude/agents/` from a Neovim command or picker integration (the plugin doesn't offer that). You manage agents by editing files directly in Neovim or asking Claude to create/edit them.

---

## 5. Comparison: Agents vs. MCP Servers vs. Plugins

| Feature | Subagents | MCP Servers | Plugins |
|---|---|---|---|
| **What** | Specialized AI assistants with custom prompts, tools, models | Tools/resources (APIs, databases, external services) exposed to Claude | Reusable automations: agents, skills, hooks, commands distributed as packages |
| **Scope** | Single session or project | Single session or project | Across sessions, teams (if published) |
| **Context** | Own isolated context window | None (MCP tool definitions are context) | Plugins can provide agents + skills + hooks that share context |
| **When to use** | Task-specific delegation (e.g., "research", "code review", "debug") | Wrapping external services (e.g., GitHub API, Slack, databases) | Reusable workflows (e.g., "code-review plugin" bundling a code-reviewer agent + review skills + hooks) |
| **Who manages** | You (edit `.claude/agents/`) or Claude | You (configure in `.mcp.json`) | Plugin author; you install with `/plugins` |
| **Tool restriction** | Yes (`tools:`, `disallowedTools:`) | No (all tools from a server are available once connected) | Yes (plugin agents can restrict tools) |

**Key distinction:** Subagents are **Claude instances**—separate AI sessions with their own reasoning. MCP servers are **tool packages**—they don't think, they just expose operations. A plugin can combine both: a plugin-distributed subagent that uses MCP servers to do work.

---

## Quick Start: Create Your First Custom Subagent

1. **Ask Claude to create it:**
   ```
   Create a personal code-improver subagent in ~/.claude/agents/ that scans files and suggests improvements for readability, performance, and best practices. Make it read-only and have it use Sonnet.
   ```

2. **Review the file** (Claude writes `~/.claude/agents/code-improver.md`):
   ```bash
   cat ~/.claude/agents/code-improver.md
   ```

3. **Invoke it:**
   - Natural language: `Use the code-improver agent to review this file`
   - @-mention: `@agent-code-improver look at the auth module`
   - Or ask Claude to suggest improvements

4. **Customize via frontmatter** if needed:
   - Change `tools:` to restrict what it can do
   - Change `model:` to route to a different model
   - Add `memory: user` for cross-session learning
   - Add `hooks:` for conditional logic

---

**Official docs:**
- Subagents: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents.md
- Run agents in parallel: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/agents.md
- How Claude Code works (agent loop): https://code.claude.com/docs/en/how-claude-code-works.md</result>

Tornato anche `claude-code-guide` con la parte operativa (verificata sui doc ufficiali). Ecco il pratico, calato sul tuo setup (Claude nel terminale via `claudecode.nvim`):

## Gestire gli agenti di Claude Code

**Dove vivono** — file Markdown con frontmatter YAML, in ordine di priorità:
- `.claude/agents/*.md` → progetto
- `~/.claude/agents/*.md` → globale (tutti i progetti)
- più quelli dei plugin installati

**Creare un agente** — due modi:
1. Chiedendolo a Claude: *"Crea un subagent `code-reviewer` in `~/.claude/agents/`, read-only, modello sonnet"* (modo consigliato).
2. Scrivendo il file a mano:
```markdown
---
name: code-reviewer
description: Revisiona il codice dopo ogni modifica. Usalo per qualità/sicurezza.
tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash
model: sonnet
---
Sei un senior code reviewer. Al richiamo: git diff, poi rivedi i file modificati...
```
Campi frontmatter principali: `name`, `description` (obbligatori), `tools`/`disallowedTools`, `model` (`sonnet`/`opus`/`haiku`/`inherit`), `permissionMode`, `maxTurns`, `background`, `isolation: worktree`, `memory`. Tabella completa: [sub-agents.md](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents.md).

**`/agents`** — nelle versioni recenti **non** apre più il wizard: stampa un promemoria a editare `.claude/agents/` o chiedere a Claude. I file sono **auto-watchati** (~3s, senza restart; restart solo la prima volta che crei la cartella).

**Invocare**:
- Linguaggio naturale: *"usa il subagent code-reviewer sulle modifiche di auth"* (Claude decide se delegare).
- `@agent-code-reviewer ...` → forza l'invocazione (typeahead con `@`).
- Sessione intera con un agente: `claude --agent code-reviewer`, oppure `{ "agent": "code-reviewer" }` in `.claude/settings.json`.

**Eseguire/osservare**:
- I subagent girano **in background di default**; `Ctrl+B` per mandare in background un task in corso; `CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_BACKGROUND_TASKS=1` per disattivare.
- `/tasks` → lista i task della sessione (controlla, attacca, ferma).
- `claude agents` → dashboard di tutte le sessioni-agente.
- Transcript per debug: `~/.claude/projects/{projectId}/{sessionId}/subagents/agent-*.jsonl`.

## Dentro Neovim (claudecode.nvim)
Tutto **identico** a shell: `claudecode.nvim` è solo un host del terminale, non tocca il sistema agenti. Apri la chat (`<leader>ai`), e lì `@agent-...`, `/tasks`, `Ctrl+B`, i file `.claude/agents/` — funzionano uguale. **Unico limite**: il plugin non offre un picker Neovim per gli agenti; li gestisci editando i file (dentro nvim) o chiedendo a Claude.

## Confine con gli altri "agenti"
- **Subagent Claude Code** = istanze Claude separate, con contesto isolato e tool ristretti (i `.claude/agents/`).
- **mcphub** (`mpc-hub.lua`) = **NON** agenti: espone **server MCP** (tool/risorse) che gli agenti richiamano.
- **avante agentic/ACP** = layer del plugin neovim, indipendente da Claude Code.

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /home/eldios/.claude/plugins/cache/claude-plugins-official/superpowers/5.1.0/skills/systematic-debugging

# Systematic Debugging

## Overview

Random fixes waste time and create new bugs. Quick patches mask underlying issues.

**Core principle:** ALWAYS find root cause before attempting fixes. Symptom fixes are failure.

**Violating the letter of this process is violating the spirit of debugging.**

## The Iron Law

```
NO FIXES WITHOUT ROOT CAUSE INVESTIGATION FIRST
```

If you haven't completed Phase 1, you cannot propose fixes.

## When to Use

Use for ANY technical issue:
- Test failures
- Bugs in production
- Unexpected behavior
- Performance problems
- Build failures
- Integration issues

**Use this ESPECIALLY when:**
- Under time pressure (emergencies make guessing tempting)
- "Just one quick fix" seems obvious
- You've already tried multiple fixes
- Previous fix didn't work
- You don't fully understand the issue

**Don't skip when:**
- Issue seems simple (simple bugs have root causes too)
- You're in a hurry (rushing guarantees rework)
- Manager wants it fixed NOW (systematic is faster than thrashing)

## The Four Phases

You MUST complete each phase before proceeding to the next.

### Phase 1: Root Cause Investigation

**BEFORE attempting ANY fix:**

1. **Read Error Messages Carefully**
   - Don't skip past errors or warnings
   - They often contain the exact solution
   - Read stack traces completely
   - Note line numbers, file paths, error codes

2. **Reproduce Consistently**
   - Can you trigger it reliably?
   - What are the exact steps?
   - Does it happen every time?
   - If not reproducible → gather more data, don't guess

3. **Check Recent Changes**
   - What changed that could cause this?
   - Git diff, recent commits
   - New dependencies, config changes
   - Environmental differences

4. **Gather Evidence in Multi-Component Systems**

   **WHEN system has multiple components (CI → build → signing, API → service → database):**

   **BEFORE proposing fixes, add diagnostic instrumentation:**
   ```
   For EACH component boundary:
     - Log what data enters component
     - Log what data exits component
     - Verify environment/config propagation
     - Check state at each layer

   Run once to gather evidence showing WHERE it breaks
   THEN analyze evidence to identify failing component
   THEN investigate that specific component
   ```

   **Example (multi-layer system):**
   ```bash
   # Layer 1: Workflow
   echo "=== Secrets available in workflow: ==="
   echo "IDENTITY: ${IDENTITY:+SET}${IDENTITY:-UNSET}"

   # Layer 2: Build script
   echo "=== Env vars in build script: ==="
   env | grep IDENTITY || echo "IDENTITY not in environment"

   # Layer 3: Signing script
   echo "=== Keychain state: ==="
   security list-keychains
   security find-identity -v

   # Layer 4: Actual signing
   codesign --sign "$IDENTITY" --verbose=4 "$APP"
   ```

   **This reveals:** Which layer fails (secrets → workflow ✓, workflow → build ✗)

5. **Trace Data Flow**

   **WHEN error is deep in call stack:**

   See `root-cause-tracing.md` in this directory for the complete backward tracing technique.

   **Quick version:**
   - Where does bad value originate?
   - What called this with bad value?
   - Keep tracing up until you find the source
   - Fix at source, not at symptom

### Phase 2: Pattern Analysis

**Find the pattern before fixing:**

1. **Find Working Examples**
   - Locate similar working code in same codebase
   - What works that's similar to what's broken?

2. **Compare Against References**
   - If implementing pattern, read reference implementation COMPLETELY
   - Don't skim - read every line
   - Understand the pattern fully before applying

3. **Identify Differences**
   - What's different between working and broken?
   - List every difference, however small
   - Don't assume "that can't matter"

4. **Understand Dependencies**
   - What other components does this need?
   - What settings, config, environment?
   - What assumptions does it make?

### Phase 3: Hypothesis and Testing

**Scientific method:**

1. **Form Single Hypothesis**
   - State clearly: "I think X is the root cause because Y"
   - Write it down
   - Be specific, not vague

2. **Test Minimally**
   - Make the SMALLEST possible change to test hypothesis
   - One variable at a time
   - Don't fix multiple things at once

3. **Verify Before Continuing**
   - Did it work? Yes → Phase 4
   - Didn't work? Form NEW hypothesis
   - DON'T add more fixes on top

4. **When You Don't Know**
   - Say "I don't understand X"
   - Don't pretend to know
   - Ask for help
   - Research more

### Phase 4: Implementation

**Fix the root cause, not the symptom:**

1. **Create Failing Test Case**
   - Simplest possible reproduction
   - Automated test if possible
   - One-off test script if no framework
   - MUST have before fixing
   - Use the `superpowers:test-driven-development` skill for writing proper failing tests

2. **Implement Single Fix**
   - Address the root cause identified
   - ONE change at a time
   - No "while I'm here" improvements
   - No bundled refactoring

3. **Verify Fix**
   - Test passes now?
   - No other tests broken?
   - Issue actually resolved?

4. **If Fix Doesn't Work**
   - STOP
   - Count: How many fixes have you tried?
   - If < 3: Return to Phase 1, re-analyze with new information
   - **If ≥ 3: STOP and question the architecture (step 5 below)**
   - DON'T attempt Fix #4 without architectural discussion

5. **If 3+ Fixes Failed: Question Architecture**

   **Pattern indicating architectural problem:**
   - Each fix reveals new shared state/coupling/problem in different place
   - Fixes require "massive refactoring" to implement
   - Each fix creates new symptoms elsewhere

   **STOP and question fundamentals:**
   - Is this pattern fundamentally sound?
   - Are we "sticking with it through sheer inertia"?
   - Should we refactor architecture vs. continue fixing symptoms?

   **Discuss with your human partner before attempting more fixes**

   This is NOT a failed hypothesis - this is a wrong architecture.

## Red Flags - STOP and Follow Process

If you catch yourself thinking:
- "Quick fix for now, investigate later"
- "Just try changing X and see if it works"
- "Add multiple changes, run tests"
- "Skip the test, I'll manually verify"
- "It's probably X, let me fix that"
- "I don't fully understand but this might work"
- "Pattern says X but I'll adapt it differently"
- "Here are the main problems: [lists fixes without investigation]"
- Proposing solutions before tracing data flow
- **"One more fix attempt" (when already tried 2+)**
- **Each fix reveals new problem in different place**

**ALL of these mean: STOP. Return to Phase 1.**

**If 3+ fixes failed:** Question the architecture (see Phase 4.5)

## your human partner's Signals You're Doing It Wrong

**Watch for these redirections:**
- "Is that not happening?" - You assumed without verifying
- "Will it show us...?" - You should have added evidence gathering
- "Stop guessing" - You're proposing fixes without understanding
- "Ultrathink this" - Question fundamentals, not just symptoms
- "We're stuck?" (frustrated) - Your approach isn't working

**When you see these:** STOP. Return to Phase 1.

## Common Rationalizations

| Excuse | Reality |
|--------|---------|
| "Issue is simple, don't need process" | Simple issues have root causes too. Process is fast for simple bugs. |
| "Emergency, no time for process" | Systematic debugging is FASTER than guess-and-check thrashing. |
| "Just try this first, then investigate" | First fix sets the pattern. Do it right from the start. |
| "I'll write test after confirming fix works" | Untested fixes don't stick. Test first proves it. |
| "Multiple fixes at once saves time" | Can't isolate what worked. Causes new bugs. |
| "Reference too long, I'll adapt the pattern" | Partial understanding guarantees bugs. Read it completely. |
| "I see the problem, let me fix it" | Seeing symptoms ≠ understanding root cause. |
| "One more fix attempt" (after 2+ failures) | 3+ failures = architectural problem. Question pattern, don't fix again. |

## Quick Reference

| Phase | Key Activities | Success Criteria |
|-------|---------------|------------------|
| **1. Root Cause** | Read errors, reproduce, check changes, gather evidence | Understand WHAT and WHY |
| **2. Pattern** | Find working examples, compare | Identify differences |
| **3. Hypothesis** | Form theory, test minimally | Confirmed or new hypothesis |
| **4. Implementation** | Create test, fix, verify | Bug resolved, tests pass |

## When Process Reveals "No Root Cause"

If systematic investigation reveals issue is truly environmental, timing-dependent, or external:

1. You've completed the process
2. Document what you investigated
3. Implement appropriate handling (retry, timeout, error message)
4. Add monitoring/logging for future investigation

**But:** 95% of "no root cause" cases are incomplete investigation.

## Supporting Techniques

These techniques are part of systematic debugging and available in this directory:

- **`root-cause-tracing.md`** - Trace bugs backward through call stack to find original trigger
- **`defense-in-depth.md`** - Add validation at multiple layers after finding root cause
- **`condition-based-waiting.md`** - Replace arbitrary timeouts with condition polling

**Related skills:**
- **superpowers:test-driven-development** - For creating failing test case (Phase 4, Step 1)
- **superpowers:verification-before-completion** - Verify fix worked before claiming success

## Real-World Impact

From debugging sessions:
- Systematic approach: 15-30 minutes to fix
- Random fixes approach: 2-3 hours of thrashing
- First-time fix rate: 95% vs 40%
- New bugs introduced: Near zero vs common
Seguo il metodo: prima le prove (Fase 1), niente fix al buio. Raccolgo in parallelo — dove è definita la derivation di gitbutler, il log completo del test fallito, e cosa hai modificato.

content.text: La build custom è in `common/nixos/overlays/gitbutler.nix`. Lo leggo per intero, prendo il log completo del test e vedo il diff di cosa hai cambiato.

Chiaro: la derivation che fallisce è **`gitbutler`** (la GUI, override di nixpkgs) — il suo `checkPhase` gira `cargo test` dell'intero workspace, e i test nuovi del crate `but` in 0.21.0 falliscono nel sandbox. La `gitbutler-cli` ha già `doCheck = false`, quindi non è lei.

Prima di decidere il fix, voglio il **perché** esatto del "No such file or directory". Leggo il log completo e cosa hai cambiato.

input.command:
cd /home/eldios/dotfiles
echo "===== what changed in the overlay (vs HEAD) ====="
git --no-pager diff HEAD -- common/nixos/overlays/gitbutler.nix 2>/dev/null | head -60
echo "(if empty, file may be committed or gitbutler-managed)"
echo
echo "===== full test log: first failure detail + sandbox context ====="
nix log /nix/store/REDACTED.21.0.drv 2>/dev/null > "$CLAUDE_JOB_DIR/tmp/gb.log"
echo "log lines: $(wc -l < "$CLAUDE_JOB_DIR/tmp/gb.log")"
echo "--- checkPhase / cargo test invocation ---"
rg -n 'checkPhase|cargo test|running .* tests|test result|doCheck|--test but|nextest' "$CLAUDE_JOB_DIR/tmp/gb.log" | head -30
input.description: Get overlay diff and full gitbutler build log

content.text: Confermato il cambiamento: sei passato da `gitbutler = super.unstable.gitbutler;` a un override completo a 0.21.0. Ora il `checkPhase` della GUI gira i test del crate `but` (472 test, 15 rossi). Serve il **perché** esatto del panic in `sandbox.rs:128`. Estraggo il dettaglio dal log.

input.command:
cd /home/eldios/dotfiles
log="$CLAUDE_JOB_DIR/tmp/gb.log"
echo "===== panics / os error / sandbox / spawn context ====="
rg -n 'panicked|os error|sandbox\.rs|No such file|Command|gix|/bin/|git\b|which|not found|CARGO_MANIFEST|assets|fixture|script' "$log" | sed -n '1,40p'
echo
echo "===== first ~35 lines of the failing test block (from line 4001) ====="
sed -n '4001,4045p' "$log"

content.text:
**Root cause trovata** (Fase 1-2 complete). Ecco la catena:

- La derivation che fallisce è la **GUI** `gitbutler` (non la CLI, che ha già `doCheck = false`). Il suo `checkPhase` gira `cargo test` sull'intero workspace: 580 unit test OK, poi i **472 test d'integrazione del crate `but`**, di cui 15 rossi.
- I 15 test falliti fanno tutti la stessa cosa: generano al volo una **fixture git** eseguendo uno script shell, perché l'archivio pre-generato non è nel sorgente. Dal log:
  > `Archive at 'tests/fixtures/generated-archives/merge-gb-local-two-branches.tar' not found, creating fixture using script 'scenario/merge-gb-local-two-branches.sh'`
- Il sorgente conferma: `sandbox.rs:128` chiama `gix_testtools::scripted_fixture_writable_with_args("scenario/<name>.sh", …).unwrap()`. Lo script usa `git`, ma nel **sandbox di build di Nix il `git` non c'è** → spawn fallisce → `os error 2` (No such file or directory) → panic.

Quindi non è un bug tuo: sono test d'integrazione della **CLI** che pretendono `git` a check-time, cosa che il sandbox non fornisce. Girano nella build della GUI solo perché `buildRustPackage` ha `doCheck` attivo di default e testa tutto il workspace.

## Fix consigliato
Disabilitare i check sulla GUI — coerente con la tua `gitbutler-cli` (già `doCheck = false`) e con la prassi nixpkgs per le app GUI. È anche **robusto ai bump** (un elenco `--skip` dei 15 test si romperebbe al prossimo aggiornamento). Applico:

    inherit version src cargoDeps;

content.text:
Verificato l'eval: il sistema valuta senza errori e `gitbutler-0.21.0` rientra nel piano di build con un **nuovo hash** (la modifica di `doCheck` ha cambiato la derivation, come atteso). Nessun errore di valutazione.

## Stato
- **Root cause**: test d'integrazione della CLII che generano fixture git via script a check-time → serve `git` nel sandbox, che non c'è → panic `os error 2`. Non un problema del tuo bump.
- **Fix**: `doCheck = false;` sulla GUI (in `common/nixos/overlays/gitbutler.nix`). Robusto ai futuri bump, coerente con `gitbutler-cli`.
- **Verificato**: eval pulito + drv ricalcolata. **Non ancora** la compilazione completa (è una build Tauri pesante, 10-30 min): la certezza finale è il build vero.

Confidenza alta sul fix: la build precedente arrivava fino al `checkPhase` (compilazione + 580 unit test già passati), e `doCheck=false` rimuove esattamente l'unico step che falliva.

## Come vuoi verificare la build vera?

Perfetto. Il fix è in place, rilancia pure `nixu` quando vuoi.

Cosa aspettarti:
- La GUI `gitbutler` ora salta il `checkPhase` (che era l'unico step rosso) → il build dovrebbe completare.
- La `gitbutler-cli` (`but`) non era coinvolta (già `doCheck = false`).
- Le altre due warning nel tuo output (`dockerfile-language-server-nodejs` rinominato, `nixfmt-rfc-style`) sono innocue e **non** bloccanti — se vuoi le sistemo a parte (sono nel tuo `neovim.nix`: `dockerfile-language-server` c'è già; il warning viene da un alias altrove).

Residuo onesto: ho verificato eval + root cause, non la compilazione finale (l'hai presa tu con nixu). Se al rebuild spunta altro, mandami il log e proseguo.

La modifica è solo in `common/nixos/overlays/gitbutler.nix` (working tree, non committata).
